title: Getting random 403 Forbidden errors on the dashboard
body: I keep getting 403 errors when I try to access the TaskFlow dashboard. It's not every time — sometimes it loads fine, sometimes I get a 403 page. It seems totally random. My permissions haven't changed and other people on my team say they're seeing it too. Started happening a couple days ago.

comments.author: triage-agent
comments.body:
Thanks for reporting this. To help narrow down what's going on:

When you hit the 403, what are you doing at that point?

1. **Just loading/refreshing the main dashboard page**
2. **Performing a specific action** (e.g., creating a task, viewing a particular project)
3. **Returning after being idle for a while**
4. **Something else**

This will help us figure out whether it's a session/auth issue vs. a backend configuration problem.

comments.author: reporter
comments.body: It's mostly #1 — just loading or refreshing the dashboard. But honestly it happens on pretty much any page, not just the dashboard. Like I'll click into a project and get a 403, then hit refresh and it loads fine. It feels random, almost like a network issue or something. I'd say maybe 1 out of 3 times it fails? It's been pretty frustrating.

comments.author: triage-agent
comments.body:
That pattern is really helpful, thanks. The intermittent nature across your whole team points toward an infrastructure issue rather than a permissions problem.

When you hit the 403, what does the error page look like?

1. **TaskFlow's styled error page** (with the app's branding/layout)
2. **A plain/generic error page** (minimal styling, maybe just "403 Forbidden" text)
3. **A page from a specific service** (e.g., Cloudflare, nginx, or another proxy name visible)
4. **Not sure / haven't looked closely**

This will tell us whether the 403 is coming from the application itself or from something in front of it (like a load balancer or CDN).

comments.author: reporter
comments.body: I think it's #1 — it looks like the normal TaskFlow error page, with the logo and everything. It's not one of those plain white pages you sometimes see. So it definitely seems like it's coming from the app itself, not like a proxy or anything.

comments.author: triage-agent
comments.body: [RESOLVED] {"title":"Intermittent 403 Forbidden errors across all pages for multiple users (~33% of requests)","problem":"Multiple team members are experiencing intermittent 403 Forbidden errors on any page in TaskFlow. The errors occur roughly 1 in 3 page loads, are not tied to any specific action or page, and resolve on refresh. The error is served by the application itself (TaskFlow-styled error page, not a proxy). The issue started approximately two days ago with no known permission changes.","root_cause_hypothesis":"One or more application instances behind a load balancer are misconfigured or running a bad deployment, causing them to reject valid authenticated requests with a 403. The ~33% failure rate suggests roughly one out of N instances is affected. A recent deployment or configuration change (approximately two days ago) likely introduced the issue on a subset of instances.","reproduction_steps":["Log in to TaskFlow as any team member","Load or refresh any page (dashboard, project view, etc.)","Repeat several times — approximately 1 in 3 loads will return a 403 Forbidden error","Refreshing the same page after a 403 will typically succeed"],"environment":"Production environment, affects multiple users across the team, started approximately 2 days ago. Error is application-level (TaskFlow-branded error page).","severity":"high","impact":"All team members are affected. Roughly 33% of page loads fail, significantly disrupting workflow. Users can work around it by refreshing, but the experience is degrading trust and productivity.","recommended_fix":"1. Check recent deployments from ~2 days ago for changes to auth middleware, session handling, or permission logic. 2. Inspect all application instances behind the load balancer — compare configs and deployed versions to identify any inconsistency. 3. Check application logs on each instance for 403 responses to correlate which instance(s) are generating them. 4. If an instance is running a bad version or config, redeploy or restart it. 5. Verify session/token validation is consistent across all instances (e.g., shared session store, consistent JWT secret).","proposed_test_case":"Send 20+ sequential authenticated requests to the dashboard endpoint and verify all return 200. Repeat while pinning requests to each individual backend instance to confirm none return 403 for valid sessions.","information_gaps":["Exact deployment or configuration change that occurred ~2 days ago","Number of application instances behind the load balancer","Server-side logs showing which instance(s) generate the 403 responses","Whether the 403 response body contains any specific error code or message beyond the styled page"]}
